title: Unfunded Mandates Accountability and Transparency Act of 2025
short_title: Unfunded Mandates Accountability and Transparency Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes government agencies do more careful studies before creating expensive new rules that cost at least one hundred million dollars each year. The agencies must talk with private businesses and small companies before making these rules. They also have to pick the option that helps people the most. If agencies don't follow these rules, people can take them to court.
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 381.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Unfunded Mandates Accountability and Transparency Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Regulatory Impact Analyses for Certain Rules
sections.text: This section changes an older law from nineteen ninety five called the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act. First, it changes the word tribal to Tribal with a capital T everywhere it appears. Second, it adds a new definition for major rule. A major rule is a government regulation that the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs decides will likely cause one of these things. It will have a yearly effect on the economy of one hundred million dollars or more. This amount will be updated every five years based on how much prices have gone up for everyday items. Or it will cause a big increase in costs or prices for consumers, individual industries, or government agencies at the federal, state, local, or tribal level. Or it will cause serious harmful effects on competition, jobs, investment, productivity, new ideas, public health and safety, or the ability of American companies to compete with foreign companies. Third, this section completely rewrites the part of the old law that deals with analyzing the impact of regulations. It requires agencies to prepare and publish two types of analysis before making any major rule. They must do an initial analysis when they first propose the rule and a final analysis when they finish the rule. The analysis must include several things. It must look at the expected benefits and costs of the rule and put numbers on them when possible. It must also look at other reasonable options the agency could choose instead, including options that use rewards and market forces to encourage good behavior, give people information so they can make their own choices, or use flexible approaches that give the most freedom in meeting the goals of the law. The analysis must explain how the rule follows the requirements in another section of the law. It must assess how much the costs to state, local, and tribal governments might be paid by federal money and whether federal resources are available to carry out the rule. It must estimate any uneven budget effects the rule might have on different regions of the country, different types of governments, different types of communities like urban or rural areas, or different parts of the private sector. It must also estimate the effect on creating or losing jobs and put numbers on this when possible. Finally, the analysis must describe how much the agency talked with elected representatives from affected governments, summarize the comments and concerns these governments shared, and explain how the agency evaluated these comments and concerns.

sections.title: Section 3 Enhanced Stakeholder Consultation
sections.text: This section changes the part of the law about getting input from people affected by regulations. It expands this to include private sector parties, including small businesses, not just government officials. The law now requires agencies to talk with these private sector parties when making federal mandates. The section also updates the guidelines for these consultations. Consultations must happen as early as possible, before the agency even publishes a proposed rule, continue through the final rule, and be built into the rulemaking process. Agencies must consult with many different state, local, and tribal officials and private sector parties including small businesses. They should consider geographic, political, and other factors that might create different points of view. Agencies should estimate benefits and costs to help with these consultations, and the amount of consultation should match how costly and important the federal mandate is. Agencies must try to get the views of governments and private sector parties on costs, benefits, and risks. They must also ask for ideas about different ways to comply and potential flexibility, and get input on whether the federal regulation will work well with similar laws at other levels of government without duplicating them. Consultations must address the combined impact of multiple regulations on the affected groups. Agencies may accept electronic comments from relevant parties but cannot use those comments as the only way to meet these consultation requirements.

sections.title: Section 4 Maximize Net Benefits or Provide Explanation
sections.text: This section replaces an old section of the law with new requirements about choosing the best regulatory option. It defines cost the same way as in the previous section. Before making any major rule that requires an impact analysis, an agency must choose the option from those they considered that provides the most net benefits. They can only consider costs and benefits that fall within the scope of the law that gives them authority to make the rule. An agency can only choose a different option if the Administrator of the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs approves it and the different option is chosen for one of two reasons. The first reason is to account for costs or benefits that cannot be measured with numbers, including costs or benefits related to constitutional or civil rights. The agency must identify all such costs and benefits and explain why they justify choosing the different option. The second reason is to achieve additional benefits or cost savings. The agency must identify all the additional benefits and their costs, or all the cost savings and their associated benefits. The agency must also explain why the additional benefits justify the extra costs or why the additional cost savings justify any benefits given up.

sections.title: Section 5 New Authorities and Responsibilities for Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs
sections.text: This section completely rewrites the responsibilities of the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs. The Administrator of this office must provide meaningful guidance and oversight to make sure that major rules requiring impact analysis follow the principles and requirements of this part of the law and other applicable laws. The Administrator must also make sure these rules do not conflict with the policies or actions of other agencies. If the Administrator determines that an agency's major rules do not comply with the law requirements, are not consistent with other laws, or conflict with other agencies' policies or actions, the Administrator must identify areas where the agency is not following the rules, notify the agency, and ask the agency to comply before finalizing the rule. The Administrator must submit a yearly written report to Congress, including specific committees in the Senate and House of Representatives. This report must detail how well each agency followed the requirements for major rules in the previous year, including activities the Administrator requested to improve compliance. The report must also include an appendix detailing how well each agency followed the consultation requirements.

sections.title: Section 6 Initiation of Rulemaking
sections.text: This section adds a new requirement to the law about starting the rulemaking process for major rules. When an agency decides to start a rulemaking that might result in a major rule, the agency must do two things. First, they must set up an electronic file system for that rulemaking, which may also have a physical version. Second, they must publish a notice in the Federal Register announcing they are starting the rulemaking process. This notice must briefly describe the subject and goals of the major rule and the problem it is trying to solve. It must refer to the legal authority the agency has to make the rule, including the specific law that allows the rulemaking. It must invite interested people to suggest alternatives and other ideas about how the agency can best accomplish its goals in the most effective way. It must tell people how they can submit written materials for the file. The notice must appear in the Federal Register no later than ninety days before the agency publishes its proposed rule.

sections.title: Section 7 Inclusion of Application to Independent Regulatory Agencies
sections.text: This section makes two changes to expand which agencies must follow these rules. First, it removes language that excluded independent regulatory agencies from the definition of agency in the Congressional Budget Act. This means independent regulatory agencies will now have to follow the unfunded mandates rules like other agencies. Second, it adds a new section to the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act that creates an exception for monetary policy. Nothing in the main parts of the law will apply to rules about monetary policy proposed or implemented by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System or the Federal Open Market Committee.

sections.title: Section 8 Judicial Review
sections.text: This section replaces the old judicial review section with new rules about court challenges. A person who is harmed by a final agency action in adopting a major rule that is subject to the impact analysis requirements can take the agency to court. They can challenge whether the agency followed the rules about preparing impact analyses, analyzing alternatives, or maximizing net benefits. The courts will follow the normal rules in federal law about the scope of review when looking at these challenges. Any court that has the power to review a rule for compliance with normal rulemaking procedures or under any other law will also have the power to review claims under this section. When granting relief in a lawsuit under this section, a court must order the agency that made the rule to take corrective action consistent with federal law.

sections.title: Section 9 Applying Substantive Point of Order to Private Sector Mandates
sections.text: This section makes a small change to the Congressional Budget Act. It expands the point of order procedure in Congress to cover all federal mandates, not just those affecting governments between different levels. This means the procedural rules that help Congress consider the costs of mandates will now also apply to mandates affecting the private sector.

sections.title: Section 10 Effective Date
sections.text: The changes made in sections three, four, five, and seven of this law and their amendments will take effect one hundred twenty days after this law is passed.
